en.extract:
The Drakensberg rockjumper or orange-breasted rockjumper (Chaetops aurantius) is a medium-sized insectivorous passerine bird endemic to the alpine grasslands and rock outcrops of the Drakensberg Mountains of southeastern South Africa and Lesotho. This taxon is closely related to the allopatric Cape rockjumper Chaetops frenatus; the two species of Chaetops are the only living members of the Chaetopidae (rockjumper family).
This rockjumper is 23–25 cm long with a long black tail and strong legs. The male has a dark grey head with a thin white supercilium and a broad white moustache. The back and wings are dark grey. The underparts are orange and the rump is rufous red. The female and juvenile have a paler grey head, upperparts and wings, a duller head pattern, an orange rump, and buff underparts. The call is a loud wheeoo. The Cape rockjumper male has rufous red underparts, and the female and young are darker buff below than in C. aurantius.
This is a ground-nesting species which forages on rocky slopes and scree. It frequently perches on rocks. Breeding is often cooperative; one or two additional individuals, usually a pair's offspring of the preceding breeding season, may assist the parents in territorial defence and alarm calling, and in the feeding of nestlings and fledglings.


Taxonomy
Chaetops aurantius was described in 1867 by Edgar Leopold Layard in his book The birds of South Africa. He placed the genus Chaetops in the thrush family. However, DNA studies have shown that the rockjumpers are not closely related to the thrushes – they are possibly basal passerida, and appear to be related to the rail-babbler and the rockfowl.
Some authorities (notably Dickinson and Christidis) treat the two rockjumpers as a single species, Chaetops frenatus, with two subspecies.


Gallery


References


External links

 Data related to Chaetops aurantius at Wikispecies
Drakensberg/Orangebreasted Rockjumper - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The '''Drakensberg rockjumper''' or '''orange-breasted rockjumper''' ('''''Chaetops aurantius''''') is a medium-sized insectivorous [[passerine]] [[bird]] endemic to the [[alpine tundra|alpine]] [[grassland]]s and rock outcrops of the [[Drakensberg|Drakensberg Mountains]] of southeastern [[South Africa]] and [[Lesotho]]. This [[taxon]] is closely related to the [[allopatric]] [[Cape rockjumper]] ''Chaetops frenatus''; the two species of ''Chaetops'' are the only living members of the [[Chaetopidae]] (rockjumper family).

This [[rockjumper]] is 23–25 cm long with a long black tail and strong legs. The male has a dark grey head with a thin white [[supercilium]] and a broad white moustache. The back and wings are dark grey. The underparts are orange and the rump is rufous red. The female and juvenile have a paler grey head, upperparts and wings, a duller head pattern, an orange rump, and [[buff (color)|buff]] [[underparts]]. The call is a loud ''wheeoo''<ref>{{cite web |url=https://animalia.bio/drakensberg-rockjumper |website=Animalia |publisher=Animalia |access-date=5 February 2026}}</ref>. The Cape rockjumper male has rufous red underparts, and the female and young are darker buff below than in ''C. aurantius''.<ref name="SASOL2002">{{cite book|last1=Sinclair|first1=Ian|last2=Hockey|first2=Phil|last3=Tarboton|first3=Warwick|title=Sasol birds of southern Africa: the region's most comprehensive illustrated guide|date=2002|publisher=Struik|location=Cape Town|isbn=978-1-86872-721-6|edition=3rd}}</ref>

This is a ground-nesting species which forages on rocky slopes and scree. It frequently perches on rocks. Breeding is often [[Cooperative breeding|cooperative]]; one or two additional individuals, usually a pair's offspring of the preceding breeding season, may assist the parents in territorial defence and alarm calling, and in the feeding of nestlings and fledglings.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Allan|first1=D.G.|editor1-last=Hockey|editor1-first=P.A.R.|editor2-last=Dean|editor2-first=W.R.J.|editor3-last=Ryan|editor3-first=P.G.|title=Roberts Birds of Southern Africa|date=2005|publisher=Trustees of the John Voelcker Bird Book Fund|location=Cape Town|isbn=978-0-620-34053-3|edition=7th|chapter=Drakensberg Rockjumper}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
''Chaetops aurantius'' was described in 1867 by [[Edgar Leopold Layard]] in his book ''The birds of South Africa''.<ref name="Layard1867">{{cite book|last1=Layard|first1=Edgar Leopold|title=The birds of South Africa: a descriptive catalogue of all the known species occurring south of the 28th parallel of south latitude|date=1867|publisher=Juta|location=Cape Town|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsofsouthafri00laya#page/126/mode/2up|access-date=16 August 2017}}</ref> He placed the genus ''Chaetops'' in the [[thrush (bird)|thrush family]]. However, DNA studies have shown that the rockjumpers are not closely related to the thrushes – they are possibly [[Basal (phylogenetics)|basal]] [[passerida]], and appear to be related to the [[rail-babbler]] and the [[Picathartes|rockfowl]].<ref name=Boyd2016>{{cite web|last1=Boyd|first1=John|title=NEORNITHES: 252 Families—Revised December 15, 2016|url=http://jboyd.net/Taxo/Families.pdf|website=jboyd.net|access-date=16 August 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=Boyd|first1=John|title=TiF Checklist: Basal Passerida|url=http://jboyd.net/Taxo/List20.html|website=jboyd.net|access-date=16 August 2017|language=en}}</ref>

Some authorities (notably [[Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World|Dickinson and Christidis]]) treat the two rockjumpers as a single species, ''Chaetops frenatus'', with two subspecies.<ref name="Lepage2017">{{cite web|last1=Lepage|first1=Denis|title=Chaetops [frenatus or aurantius] (Rock Jumper) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=04744EFD&sec=taxontable|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org|access-date=9 September 2017}}</ref>
